figures.caption: Figure 10: Qualitative results of four baselines and DisINR for sparse-view CT with 60 projection views on a representative sample of the unseen COVID-19 dataset [31].
Figure 4: Qualitative results of five baselines and DisINR for sparse-view CT with 60 projection views on a representative sample of the DeepLesion dataset [47].
Table 3: Quantitative results of five baselines and DisINR for sparse-view CT on the DeepLesion [47] and LIDC [1] datasets.
Table 8: Quantitative results of five baselines and DisINR for sparse-view CT with 60 and 90 projection views on the unseen COVID-19 dataset [31] containing abnormal anatomical structures.
Figure 9: Qualitative results of four baselines and DisINR for undersampled MRI with a radial pattern of AF = 10× on a representative sample of the fastMRI-T2w dataset [17].

Figure 1: Overview of the proposed DisINR, which consists of a shared encoder–decoder pair (fϕ, gψ) and multiple subject-specific encoders {fφi }Ni=1. A) Pre-training Stage: All modules in DisINR are jointly optimized from a few raw measurements Y = {yi}Ni=1 by incorporating differentiable forward models {Ai}Ni=1. B) Test-time Adaption: Given a new measurement y /∈ Y and the corresponding forward model A, the shared pair (fϕ∗ , gψ∗) is frozen while only the subjectspecific encoder fφ is optimized. This effectively injects learned population priors into the INR optimization process, enabling faster and higher-quality reconstruction x.

Figure 5: Visualization of DisINR pretraining on the fastMRI-T2w dataset [17]: A) Prior dataset, mean image of all prior samples. B) Prior image #18. C) Reconstructed image #18 by DisINR. D) Individual feature extracted by the subject-specific encoder of DisINR for sample #18. E) Shared feature extracted by the shared encoder of DisINR from the prior dataset.
Figure 13: Qualitative results of five baselines and our DisINR for sparse-view CT with 60 projection views on two representative samples of the DeepLesion [47] and LIDC [1] datasets.
Figure 6: Qualitative results of two baselines and DisINR ablating frozen components for sparse-view CT with 60 projection views on a representative sample of the DeepLesion dataset [47].
Figure 2: Qualitative result (A) on a representative case and optimization curves (B) over all cases of three SOTA INR baselines and DisINR for 3D CT volume fitting from the AAPM dataset [22].

Figure 12: Quantitative comparison of five baselines and our DisINR for undersampled MRI with a Cartesian pattern of AF = 6× on two representative samples of the fastMRI-T2w and fastMRI-FLAIR datasets [17].
